title: Write What I Believe in 200 Words
subtitle: A journal practice for getting your own spiritual position onto the page
excerpt: A journal practice for writing down what you actually believe, in plain language, without needing it to fit any tradition. For people who have a spiritual life that resists easy description and have never tried to put it into words.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you have never written down what you believe and want a starting point, or when your beliefs have shifted and you want to know where you actually stand.

content.text: A journal practice for putting your spiritual position into plain words. Not a theology essay. Two hundred words, written in first person, saying what you believe about the world beyond the material, what happens when people die, what gives meaning to the ordinary. You don't need to get it right. You need to start somewhere.
When have you felt certain that something exists beyond the material? Describe the moment.
One specific instance. A place, a moment, a person, something in nature. Write what it felt like and why it felt like more than coincidence or chemistry.
What do you believe happens when people die?
Write what you actually think, not what you were taught. If you don't know, write what you suspect or hope. Both are legitimate starting points.
What gives the ordinary things meaning for you?
Not the dramatic moments. The regular day. What makes it feel like it counts.
Write two hundred words of what you believe, in first person, starting with 'I believe'.
Use the warm-up to find the content. Don't edit while you write. Two hundred words is not much. It is enough to make a start.
